planting.note: Plant rooted hardwood cuttings or container shrubs in fall or very early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; white currant is the mildest-flavored of the currants, with translucent, pearl-like berries that are sweet enough to eat fresh.

directions: Plant rooted hardwood cuttings or container-grown shrubs in fall or very early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; white currant fruits best with some afternoon shade in warm climates and benefits from a cross-pollinator though it is largely self-fertile. Ribes sativum in its white-fruited forms (often grouped with R. rubrum) produces distinctive, translucent, pearl-white to pale-pink berries with a milder, less tart flavor than red currants, making them more suitable for fresh eating and elegant as a garnish on desserts; they are excellent for jelly, wine, and as a decoration for cakes and pastries. White currant is one of the most attractive of all fruiting shrubs when in fruit — the translucent clusters catch the light beautifully. 'Blanka', 'Primus', and 'White Versailles' are classic cultivars available from specialty nurseries.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), peaty soil in full sun to partial shade; bog bilberry grows in subarctic and subalpine bogs and moist tundra and is the most cold-hardy edible Vaccinium available.

directions: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), peaty soil in full sun to partial shade; bog bilberry absolutely requires constantly moist to wet, strongly acidic, poorly drained peaty or boggy soil and will not succeed in drier, neutral conditions. Vaccinium uliginosum is a circumboreal subarctic and subalpine species found in bogs, tundra, and moist mountain heaths across North America, Europe, and Asia — the hardiest of all Vaccinium, surviving temperatures to -60°F; it produces small, round, blue-black berries with a sweet, mild flavor and exceptionally high antioxidant content (higher than commercial blueberries). The berries are an important subsistence food for northern indigenous peoples and are used in jams, pies, and fresh eating. Best suited to cold, wet, acidic sites in zones 1–6 where it is invaluable for bog gardens and moist native plantings.
sources: University of Alaska Fairbanks Cooperative Extension

planting.note: Plant rooted runners or divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; green strawberry is a slow spreader that forms small, tight clumps rather than the aggressive runners of F. vesca.

directions: Plant rooted runners or divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; green strawberry spreads much more slowly than wood strawberry (F. vesca) and forms compact, slowly spreading clumps rather than long-running stolons. Fragaria viridis is a European native wild strawberry distinguished from the more common F. vesca by its fruits, which remain green even when fully ripe (turning creamy-white to slightly pinkish), and by its distinctively sweet, pineapple-like aroma that some find even more delicious than common wild strawberries. The berries are small (under 1/2 inch) but pack an extraordinary fragrance and flavor; they do not separate cleanly from the calyx when picked (unlike F. vesca) and are best eaten fresh directly from the plant. An excellent collector's plant and conversation piece in the fruit garden, though not productive enough for large-scale harvest.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root trees in spring in any well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; European hackberry is an extremely adaptable, drought-tolerant, and long-lived tree excellent for difficult urban and dry landscape sites.

directions: Plant container-grown or bare-root trees in spring in any well-drained, alkaline to acidic, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; European hackberry is remarkably adaptable to poor, dry, rocky soils, alkaline conditions, and urban pollution. Celtis australis is a long-lived (500+ year), medium to large tree of the Mediterranean basin and southern Europe, producing small (1/3 inch), round fruits that turn from yellow to orange to dark-purple-black when fully ripe in autumn, with a sweet, date-like flavor and a single large seed; the fruits were widely eaten by Mediterranean peoples since antiquity (the "lotus" fruit referenced by Homer that caused forgetfulness, though the identity of Homer's lotus is debated). The tree's beautiful, long-petioled, rough-textured leaves, tolerance of drought and heat, and picturesque form make it an excellent ornamental in zones 7–11; it is one of the best shade trees for very hot, dry, alkaline landscapes.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container shrubs in spring in well-drained, alkaline to slightly acidic, lean soil in full sun; common barberry is very drought-tolerant once established but is an alternate host for wheat stem rust and is banned or restricted in some grain-producing states — check local regulations before planting.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container shrubs in spring in well-drained, lean, alkaline to slightly acidic soil in full sun; common barberry is drought-tolerant and adaptable to poor soils but is an alternate host for wheat stem rust (Puccinia graminis) and is prohibited in many wheat-growing states — confirm legality before planting. Berberis vulgaris is a thorny, deciduous European shrub producing clusters of small, oval, bright-red berries (barberries) in fall with an intensely tart, citrusy, slightly astringent flavor rich in vitamin C; they are used in Persian cooking (zereshk polo — barberry rice pilaf) where they are a quintessential flavoring, and for making barberry jam, juice, wine, and candy throughout Europe and western Asia. The bright-yellow wood and bark contain berberine, a traditional medicinal compound. The berries are also an important food source for birds in winter.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in fall in very well-drained, sandy to rocky, slightly acidic, infertile soil in full sun; common manzanita is native to California's foothills and requires excellent drainage and dry summers — it will rot in humid, wet conditions.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in fall in very well-drained, sandy to rocky, slightly acidic, infertile soil in full sun; manzanita absolutely requires excellent drainage and summer drought — it will not survive in humid climates or wet soils and is best suited to Mediterranean-climate gardens in California and similar dry-summer climates. Arctostaphylos manzanita is a large, beautiful, evergreen California native shrub or small tree with signature smooth, mahogany-red bark that peels to reveal bright, polished inner bark; it produces masses of urn-shaped, pink-white flowers beloved by hummingbirds and native bees in late winter through early spring, followed by small (1/3 inch), round, apple-like fruits (manzanita means "little apple" in Spanish) that ripen red to dark burgundy in summer. The edible fruits are tart and mealy, used by California Native Americans for making a refreshing cider-like beverage by soaking in water, and for making pinole (ground dried fruit). The exfoliating bark is one of the most beautiful features of any native western shrub.
